talk_title: Genomic characterization of multiple primary lung cancers in TRACERx

speaker.name: Charlotte Grieco
speaker.affiliation: Cancer Research UK, Francis Crick Institute, London, UK

MOA_summary: Whole-exome sequencing of multiple primary NSCLC tumors revealed a 10% discordance between clinical and genomic relatedness, indicating many cases are misclassified as metastases or primaries, with no distinct genomic features distinguishing multiple primaries.
key_findings: 25% of TRACERx patients have >1 primary tumor, 12% have >1 lung primary
10% discordance between clinical and genomic relatedness leads to potential mistreatment
Multiple primaries show similar genomic divergence as tumors from different patients
Higher cumulative tobacco exposure associated with multiple lung primaries
open_questions: What host immune factors influence independent tumor initiation within the same patient?

talk_title: Mouse Cancer Cell Line Atlas reveals tissue-specific KRAS dosage evolution

models.name: Mouse Cancer Cell Line Atlas (590 lines)
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS-driven mouse lines from pancreas, lung, intestine

MOA_summary: Analysis of 590 mouse cancer cell lines shows that KRAS dosage increase (gain of a second mutant allele) is a common event across KRAS-driven tumors, occurring early in pancreatic cancer but later in lung and intestinal cancers, with tissue-specific dependencies on CDKN2A or Wnt pathway alterations.
key_findings: Two-thirds of KRAS-driven mouse pancreatic cancers acquire a second mutant KRAS copy early in tumor evolution
KRAS dosage increase occurs late (carcinoma stage) in lung and intestinal KRAS-driven cancers
CDKN2A loss is required for KRAS dosage gain in pancreas but not in intestine or lung
Wnt pathway activation precedes KRAS amplification in intestinal tumors
open_questions: How do KRAS dosage dynamics translate to human tumor evolution across tissues?

talk_title: Allele-specific KRAS mutation signatures in lung adenocarcinoma
MOA_summary: Signature analysis of 100k whole-genome lung adenocarcinomas shows smoking-associated SBS4 directly causes KRAS G12C, G12V, and G13C mutations, while clock-like signatures drive other KRAS variants, revealing subtype-specific mutational etiologies.
key_findings: Smoking signature SBS4 directly implicated in KRAS G12C, G12V, G13C mutations
Clock-like signatures SBS5/40 associated with KRAS G12A, G12D, G13D, Q61H mutations
KRAS mutant tumors show higher smoking signature burden than KRAS-wt
Subtype-specific signature contributions suggest smoking does not drive all KRAS variants
open_questions: Can signature-based attribution inform personalized prevention or early detection strategies?

talk_title: Functional consequences of ecDNA versus HSR amplification architecture

speaker.name: Elizabeth Brunk
speaker.affiliation: University of North Carolina Chapel Hill, USA

models.name: ERBB2-amplified ecDNA model (DepMap line)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 3 Mb ecDNA with ERBB2, high copy number heterogeneity

MOA_summary: Single-cell multi-omics of ecDNA- versus HSR-amplified cell lines shows that ecDNA dosage correlates tightly with gene expression and protein levels, with nonlinear transcriptional scaling for specific genes (e.g., PVT1), and that ecDNA heterogeneity is actively maintained through biased inheritance and growth advantage.
key_findings: ecDNA-bearing cells display a broad 100‑1000 copy number range per cell, correlating with HER2 protein levels
Gene expression scaling with ecDNA dosage is stronger and more coordinated than in HSR cells
PVT1 transcriptional output and Myc program are highly dependent on ecDNA architecture
Sorted high‑ecDNA and low‑ecDNA populations reconverge to original distribution within 2‑3 generations via biased segregation and growth advantage
open_questions: What therapeutic strategies can exploit the dependence of specific oncogenic programs on ecDNA architecture?

talk_title: Single-cell dissection of lung adenocarcinoma to small cell transformation

speaker.name: Joseph Chen
speaker.affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, USA

models.name: 47 tumors from 24 patients with transformed or combined neuroendocrine histology
models.genotype_or_key_features: EGFR mutant and wild-type, p53/RB1 loss, APOBEC hypermutation

clinical_data.value: poor, approaching de novo small cell

MOA_summary: Single-cell RNA and spatial profiling reveal that adenocarcinoma cells transition through a basal, hypoxia‑driven intermediate state before diverging to small‑cell neuroendocrine or squamous lineages, with FOX2B driving neuroendocrine reprogramming and loss of NOTCH signaling.
key_findings: Transformed tumors display greater phenotypic diversity than de novo small cell
A basal intermediate state marked by NF‑κB, JAK‑STAT, hypoxia precedes neuroendocrine transformation
FOXF2B overexpression induces neuroendocrine markers and suppresses EGFR signaling
SDK1 and KEAP1 mutations may define a neuroendocrine‑to‑GI plasticity axis in large‑cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
open_questions: Can targeting the basal hypoxic program prevent lineage plasticity and improve outcomes?

talk_title: Optical pooled CRISPRi screen identifies regulators of pancreatic cancer plasticity

models.name: Patient-derived PDAC line with inducible CRISPRi

MOA_summary: Lineage‑resolved optical pooled CRISPRi screening of ~900 perturbations in PDAC cells identifies H3K9 methyltransferases SUV39H1/2 as plasticity regulators that increase epithelial‑mesenchymal transitions via heterochromatin loss, distinct from classic EMT drivers.
key_findings: ZEB1 knockdown biases cells toward epithelial state regardless of initial phenotype
GRL2 loss biases toward mesenchymal state
SUV39H1/2 loss enhances bidirectional plasticity, increasing state transitions
Dual‑guide approach enables detection of epistatic interactions (e.g., ZEB1 vs GRL2)
open_questions: Can pharmacologic inhibition of SUV39H1/2 modulate PDAC plasticity in vivo?

talk_title: Joint single-cell methylome and 3D genome profiling of glioma heterogeneity

speaker.name: Yue Wu
speaker.affiliation: University of California San Diego / Salk Institute, USA

MOA_summary: Joint single-cell methylome and chromatin contact profiling of gliomas reveals asynchronous changes where 3D genome reorganization precedes DNA methylation shifts, identifies mismatched OPC‑like/astrocyte‑like states, and shows ecDNA subclones (e.g., EGFRvIII) associate with distinct methylation programs.
key_findings: IDH‑mutant gliomas share clonal astrocyte‑like cells with 1p/19q codeletion
Mismatched methylation and 3D genome states suggest early chromatin remodeling during cell‑state transitions
ecDNA (EGFR, CDK4) present only in IDH‑wt GBM, with structural variants correlating with methylation subclones
Oncogene‑specific TF motif enrichment (MAPK for EGFR ecDNA, Hedgehog for GLI1 ecDNA) links ecDNA structure to epigenetic reprogramming
open_questions: How does ecDNA‑driven methylation remodeling influence therapeutic response in GBM?
